comments.title: amulet autotoxicity
comments.content:
I've had great success using the red-bag, salt and nail amulet you describe in the FAQ and have employed a new bad consistently every two to tree weeks for the past year or so. Something I noticed today is that while wearing it I felt really, really bad. I already had felt that it was no longer so effective, and so I thought that I should change it, but kept on forgetting. As the day wore on I felt worse and worse until I took it off. After taking off the old amulet I felt immediately better. I disposed of it in the normal way and made a new one, and did extensive purificatory rites and now feel fine.

If the amulet absorbs sufficient nasties, does it start to backflow into the etheric body of the person wearing it? That's what it felt like was going on today. I'm also interested if other folks have experienced this effect.

replies.title: Re: amulet autotoxicity
replies.content: Yes, if the salt absorbs enough nasty energy it begins to radiate it. You must be spending time in spaces where the etheric energy is really toxic!

replies.title: Re: amulet autotoxicity
replies.content: What's the best way to dispose of such things?

replies.title: Re: amulet autotoxicity
replies.content: Bury it in the ground, preferably in your front yard. (In hoodoo tradition, you bury something in the front yard if you want the energies to disperse, in the back yard if you want them to stay put.) If you can't do that, get cold water running in your sink, untie the bag, and pour the salt into the water so it's dissolved and carried away down the drain -- cold water absorbs etheric energy very effectively. Wash the nail in the cold water and then throw it away, along with the cloth.

replies.title: Re: amulet autotoxicity
replies.content:
ArchDruid,
I am not a gardener, but I recall hearing something about salt hurting plants. Is this a worry if I bury the used salt in my front yard?
Thank you.

replies.title: Re: amulet autotoxicity
replies.content: Small amounts of salt won't be a problem unless you live in a very arid region, and the amulet shouldn't have more than a teaspoon in it.

replies.title: Re: amulet autotoxicity
replies.content: What is the proper way to dispose of one of those?

replies.title: Re: amulet autotoxicity
replies.content: See above.

replies.title: Re: amulet autotoxicity
replies.content:
I have made up an amulet using a red bag I bought on the internet which is made of a polyester flock material. Would it work better it was made from cotton / flannel?

Does the bag need to be washed regularly or do you just need to change the salt and nail? Also would it help if I put a rowan berry in there too?

replies.title: Re: amulet autotoxicity
replies.content: The cloth shouldn't matter, as long as it isn't silk or linen (which are etheric insulators). Wash the bag if it looks or feels dirty. As for the rowan berry, no, save that for a different amulet -- this one is very specific, and shouldn't be combined with other forms of natural magic.

replies.title: Re: amulet autotoxicity

replies.title: Re: amulet autotoxicity
replies.content: Happens all the time. Magic is simply the systematic application of things that are hardwired into our minds and bodies, and plenty of people broadcast nasty influences who have never so much as opened a book on magic.

comments.title: SoP again

replies.title: Re: SoP again
replies.content: Of course you can do it while still, when you're sick or when some other factor makes it better to do it that way. In fact, it's a good exercise now and then to visualize yourself doing the ritual, imagining every detail as vividly as possible, as though you were actually doing it physically. There are few better ways to develop the capacity to visualize, and with practice it becomes as effective to do it that way as to do it on the material plane.

comments.title: Horoscope Correction
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

If this is not too much to ask:

I’m trying to find my friend’s ascendant. He was lost at sea in a floating mattress at age 6, floating far from the coast, and being rescued last moment by sheer luck. Would you look for something with Neptune crossing a main axis in a progressed horoscope?

Thank you very much,

Njura

replies.title: Re: Horoscope Correction
replies.content:
Despite the name, Neptune doesn't rule oceans -- the Moon does. In a horoscope like that, I'd expect to see the Moon very strongly placed in the chart for the sixth day of your friend's life, and there should be multiple major aspects for that year which more or less describe the event. (Say, Moon squaring the ruler of your friend's natal ascendant and Jupiter trine your friend's natal Sun, or something like that.)

To doublecheck, if you know the date when the incident occurred, cast a chart for the place it happened for that day. There should be major aspects between the planets on that day and the planets and angles of your friend's natal chart.

replies.title: Re: Horoscope Correction
replies.content: That's fascinating, thanks a lot! Do transits play a role as well, or just progressions? And do you know of a book or other website for more details (so we don't bother you all the time...)?

replies.title: Re: Horoscope Correction
replies.content: Yes, you should use transits -- that's a very common way to check your chart rectification. If an outer planet transits an important natal planet the same year the progression indicates the same event, you've probably scored a hit. The book I use for chart rectification is Laurie Efrein's How to Rectify a Birthchart; there's also a useful section on rectification in Llewellyn George's original A-Z Horoscope Maker and Delineator (which, btw, is out of copyright -- somebody ought to bring out a new edition!)

replies.title: Re: Horoscope Correction
replies.content: Maybe if you write a new foreword, and include some annotations on it, you might be able to convince one of your publishers to bring a new edition forth.

replies.title: Re: Horoscope Correction
replies.content: Hmm -- very possibly a workable plan. It would probably have to be retitled, since Llewellyn has a lot invested in their bowdlerized New A-Z book, and it would need some comments on Pluto, Ceres, Chiron, and the other minor planets -- but yes, that might be a possibility.

replies.title: Re: Horoscope Correction
replies.content:
New is always good, right? It was their way to copyright their altered version.

Name it Traditional, Original, or Real A-Z and you are set.

replies.title: Re: Horoscope Correction
replies.content: I’d be interested in that. I just bought a copy of the “new” version, published in 1985 or so, I think. I recalled your words of caution about that version, but I’m trying to learn the basics of astrology, and it was only 50 cents at the thrift store!! What are the main problems with that edition? I’m hoping it’s not too flawed to serve as an overview of basic astrological concepts...

replies.title: Re: Horoscope Correction
replies.content: It's a perfectly good overview of basic astrological concepts; it's just been revised to suit the modern fixation on the astrology of personality, and leaves out some things I find useful.

replies.title: Re: Horoscope Correction
replies.content: Looks like a good way to learn about the effects that future Transits and progressions could have as well!

replies.title: Re: Horoscope Correction
replies.content: Ding! We have a winner. I track my transits daily and my progressions annually -- and it was after I started doing that, and using transits to time such things as when to submit a proposal to a publisher, that my career really took off. Just a hint...

replies.title: Re: Horoscope Correction
replies.content: Hint taken here.  I managed to find a 1973 edition of the book - in really good condition at time of purchase ... a decade from now, who knows?  It definitively has the thickness to be a textbook.

replies.title: Re: Horoscope Correction
replies.content: Did you select a good date / time astrologically when you got married? What would you look for to pick the right date?

replies.title: Re: Horoscope Correction
replies.content:
I barely knew the first thing about astrology when Sara and I got married, but clearly we did something right. Electional astrology is a fairly complex branch of the art, and when it's a matter of timing a marriage, you have to correlate the natal horoscopes of the two people involved with what's actually happening in the sky at that moment -- something that takes a fair amount of experience! If you're looking to work out the right time for a marriage, I'd encourage you to find a local astrologer who does that kind of work and have her* elect a date for you.

(*"Her" because in the US, at least, the great majority of practicing astrologers are women.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yes, very much so. Company of houses doesn't perfect a chart by itself -- it just opens up another possibility for perfection.

"Prove It To Me" is a classic interpersonal game in the Transactional Analysis sense of the term, and a lot of people play it inside their own heads.

comments.title: Hypnosis and etheric crud
comments.content:
When I was 10 and my sister 12, we attended a couple sleep-overs with a neighbor who was also 12. There were a couple other guests. Our friend introduced us to a "game" that I think was some sort of hypnosis. In a nearly dark room, she would have a volunteer lie down on the floor and rub their temples to relax them as she told them a long story that was always ended with how the volunteer murdered their boyfriend in a fit of jealous rage. I don't think any of these girls even had any sort of boyfriend yet, but they really took in the story, screamed, frantically cried with real tears, snot, and red faces, and had to be assured that they had not really murdered anyone.

I volunteered once to be the subject, but told myself I would not let the story affect me. Really, that was easy as the stories were very hokey to me, especially as romantic relationships were the furthest thing from my mind at that age. So I was a dud as far as this party game went.

Fifty years later, though, I still feel sorry that I even participated as an observer of the "game." It was traumatic to those girls, and I understood it to be a cruelty at the time. I always hoped that the cruel stories did not stay with the girls somehow, burrowing into their brains forever. It seems like party-trick type hypnosis is supposed to have a signal to end the game, to turn it off so to speak. As far as I can tell, there was no end signal to come out of the trance, other than the girls' own trauma, like waking from a nightmare. So I fear the suggestion, the accusation, might have long been in them, needling and indicting.

You've used the phrase "etheric crud". I'm thinking this kind of game has added to the etheric crud out there, and that little waves of it wash up on unknown beaches. Make sense?

replies.title: Re: Hypnosis and etheric crud
replies.content: That's astral crud rather than etheric crud. The etheric level is the level of life force; the astral level is the level of imagery, emotion, and most ordinary kinds of thinking. With that amendment, though, yes, it makes perfect sense -- and that's a ghastly thing to do to a young person.

replies.title: Re: Hypnosis and etheric crud
replies.content:
This reminds me of a game that was really popular at sleepovers when I was young. One girl would lay down, surrounded by the other girls. A storyteller would rub the laying girl's temples and tell the story of how she would die (in the future - could be old age or not, but the idea was to be creative or outlandish or funny). Then everyone would chant "Stiff as a board, light as a feather, dead, dead, gone" and try to lift the girl only using the first two fingers of each hand.

What was going on there? Any crud I should try to dispel at this point, 30 years later?

replies.title: Re: Hypnosis and etheric crud
replies.content:
In the version I heard of -- it was always a girls' thing, so I never got to try it in this life -- the girl then rose up into the air with only the slightest effort. Did that happen in your experience?

So long as you didn't freak out, you should be fine. You will die, you know, and getting used to the idea is a good thing.

replies.title: Re: Hypnosis and etheric crud
replies.content:
It was done at almost every slumber party - I remember at least once the girl seemed quite light and we could lift her effortlessly. But usually nothing happened.

I do feel a bit guilty looking back, as I was typically the one picked to tell the death story as I made them nice and juicy. I was also always the pretend fortune teller. Maybe that's why I found my way here so many years later!

-lp

comments.content:

I have been thinking about winding up my job (bureaucracy a oriented, pays the mortgage) in order to devote my time, my energies and my learning entirely to my clinic (a vocation, which earns a small amount, but mostly "orients" me in the world in a wholesome way.

2. Time & day of the week? Will this make a difference?

replies.title: Re: An auspicious day to retire, but not retire
replies.content: Look at your natal chart, and choose a day when a waxing Moon is applying by trine, sextile, or conjunction to the natal position of the planet that rules your ascendant or tenth house, and none of the malefic planets are applying by square or opposition to any planet in your natal chart. (Separating aspects are fine; they represent something that's passing off.) That's going to be a good day to make any significant change.

replies.content:
Thank you very much.

Good morning, and thank you for hosting Magic Mondays!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The reason for the square form is very simple. Back before you could get printed astrological charts, you had to draw the chart form by hand. It's the easiest thing in the world to draw the square form using compass and straightedge; it's a little more complicated to do the circular form, and so the square form was common as a convenience. Once cheap printing became available and you could buy pads of horoscope forms from astrologers' publishing houses, the circular form became more common because it's easier to read aspects at a glance.

I don't know of a source off hand that covers the history of horoscope forms; good histories of astrology should discuss it, but I'm far from sure if there are a lot of those!

comments.title: French Druidry
comments.content: In your Druidry Handbook you briefly discuss that French Druids made a large contribution. I admit I haven’t gone through the further reading sections, but are there books discussing Druidry in France? Could you give some titles, or discuss French Druidry here a little?

replies.title: Re: French Druidry
replies.content:
Sure. The French Druid scene is, well, French -- meaning that it's strongly influenced both by other trends in French occultism, and by the French tendency to factionalism. (I think it was Charles de Gaulle who once said in despair, "How can one govern a nation that has three thousand varieties of cheese?") So the Breton Druids generally won't talk to the French Druids, and there are various groups of Breton Druids and French Druids alike who won't talk to each other either, and competing books teach diametrically opposed teachings, and all the usual backing and forthing you already know about if you're familiar with French politics or, for that matter, French Freemasonry.

As for books, good heavens, yes. Among those I've read are Robert Ambelain's Les Traditions Celtiques and a flurry of books by Paul and Rene Bouchet, of which Les Druides is probably the most widely available. There are scores of others, ranging from the thoughtful to the giddy.

replies.title: Re: French Druidry
replies.content: Dear gods: the factionalism of the French mixed with the contrariness of the Druids must produce something spectacular....

replies.title: Re: French Druidry
replies.content:
Oh, it does! There's some amazingly good stuff in French Druid literature, and some amazingly silly stuff -- I'm thinking here about an earnest discussion in one such book of two additional moons of Earth which orbit inside the planet, and influence certain regions of the Earth's surface by their orbits.

Then there's the stuff that's both. My favorite Do-In author, Jacques de Langre, wrote in English but came out of the French Druid scene; his practical instructions for Do-In technique are peerless, and his account of the theory behind it all (in Do-In 2) is loop-the-loop loopy. Jean Rofidal is saner but his instructions are to my mind less useful.

comments.title: Mediums/Contacting the dead
comments.content:
There's a question I have been meaning to ask for a while, but today is the first Monday I've been able to. It's mainly what you feeling are regarding mediums. As I have mentioned, I had a particularly traumatic childhood, but at the same time was close to my father (who was more like a brother) and my grandmother, who raised and adopted me. My grandmother died in the mid-1990s while I was living in Scotland, and I knew when she died, I literally felt it. She has come to me a few times over the years, almost like a "fairy grandmother".

My father disappeared for good when my grandmother died. He was a heroin addict from before I was born, spent much of my childhood in and out of prison, and the only reason he wasn't homeless was because of my grandmother. When she died, he finally did become permanently homeless, I was in my early 20s, on a year abroad, completely broke living on student loans and homeless on and off myself. I looked for him on and off over the years, but due to the HIPPA laws and his homelessness I always lost the trail. My horrible aunt and cousin kept telling me he was dead, but I could feel that he wasn't. 2 years ago the County of Los Angeles found me via social media and reached out saying he was in a nursing home in Hollywood dying, requesting I apply legally for conservatorship. It's all very complicated legally...so my husband and I raced down to LA to see him before they moved him and I lost track again. He was about 100lbs, in a fetal position and basically in a coma like state, on feeding tubes, etc. Because I didn't have legal rights, they wouldn't tell me anything. All I know is he spent 20+ years on and off the streets of LA, and in and out of hospitals due to severe mental and physical health issues. He finally died last year, and I paid for his cremation, and have him in an urn in my backyard with a makeshift shrine to him with all the rocks and shells I collected over the past 30 years (he loved the ocean, and nature).

That's all probably more than you need to know, but I think it matters that he lived such a tormented life. He was such an important part of my childhood, and despite all that happened, he was a very special person to me and a huge influence. And while I could feel he was alive all these years, now I feel nothing, and have so many questions. I have thought about going to see a medium, but two things scare me. One is, maybe it's best to not reach out, and it's best to just let him be? Maybe that's why I don't feel anything? The second is, if it would be OK to reach out, how on earth would I find someone that's a real medium that could handle something like this? I really feel like I need some sort of closure with my father, but perhaps there is a better way to seek it?

Tude

replies.title: Re: Mediums/Contacting the dead
replies.content:
I don't recommend using mediums. There are some honest ones, but the great majority are dishonest and use various tricks to establish and maintain their clienteles. M. Lamar Keene's book The Psychic Mafia, by a corrupt medium who finally couldn't live with himself and spilled the beans on the spiritualist racket, is worth reading in this context.

What I'd recommend is to simply maintain the shrine you've created, and talk to him when you feel like it. He'll be working his way through the afterlife process, absorbing the lessons of the life just past, and your love will help him in that process. If you have a relationship with a deity, prayer is another really good way to help a dead person work through the process. Closure isn't really an option in our world; the best you can do is to help, and let things unfold.

comments.title: Active and Passive Spirit

replies.title: Re: Active and Passive Spirit
replies.content:
There isn't a lot of commentary on Active and Passive Spirit in the GD system, just the pentagrams and certain rules about using them. In GD practice you invoke Passive Spirit in the Supreme Ritual of the Pentagram before the passive elements (water and earth) and Active Spirit before the active elements (fire and air); you also use them in a few other advanced rituals, but those seem to be mostly riffing off the Pentagram ritual.

comments.title: Some polytheistic questions!
comments.content:
Good morning JMG!

1) I'm curious about the logistics of worshiping localized gods in a global context. Boann and Hapi, for instance, are tied to specific rivers in Ireland/Egypt respectively, and yet they're worshiped by pagans who live thousands of miles away from those rivers. How does this work, exactly? I've always been told that the gods aren't omnipresent in the way the Christian god is purported to be. Is this just a case of time and space working differently on a divine level?

2) Is it possible and/or advisable to "trial run" a relationship with a deity? To set forth a specific period of time in which one would honor that god, and after which point the agreement dissolves and both parties go their separate ways? Not sure if this would come across as rude to the god in question...

3) In your opinion, what is Jesus "the god of," so to speak? Always curious to hear what domain other polytheists place Jesus in.

One last point of unrelated curiosity: are you familiar with John Beckett? He seems to be a fairly big name in modern druidry and Celtic polytheism, so I figured it was possible that the two of you may have crossed paths before. Wondered if you had any thoughts (positive or negative) about his perspectives on druidry, polytheism, or both.

Many thanks!

replies.title: Re: Some polytheistic questions!
replies.content:
1) We don't know a lot about gods, and the logistics of worship is one of the things we know very, very little about. Some gods are omnipresent, or at they might as well be -- Hu the Mighty, for example, seems to be equally present in every place I've ever been. Others are more localized. Why? Good question.

2) Well, would you do that if you were having a relationship with a human being? Not outside of prostitution, I hope. The same principles apply to a relationship with a god. Such relationships aren't necessarily permanent, and can be ended by either party, but it's not a matter of contracts and scheduling but of mutual attachment.

3) I've always thought of him as just another Middle Eastern death-and-resurrection deity, along the lines of Tammuz, Adonis, Attis, et al. (They ought to form a union; Persephone et al. could organize the ladies' auxiliary.) Jesus is distinct from others of the same class by being a god of unconditional love rather than a god of vegetation, so his worship is more about morality than fertility, but other than that, it's pretty much the same.

4) John Beckett is a former student of mine, and for that reason I don't really think it's appropriate for me to comment on his activities. I will say that we're no longer in contact.

replies.content: Are there pagan gods of unconditional love like Jesus? That is something I would like to be there as part of a pantheon but, as far as I know the major traditional pantheons don't have one. I struggle with this aspect of paganism. The idea of different gods for different things makes sense to me, and I feel drawn to ancient gods who are connected to aspects of nature. But there doesn't seem to be a way to worship or call to that unconditional love within the same ritual as other gods, as I would really like to, because of the advice not to mix pantheons.

replies.title: Re: Some polytheistic questions!
replies.content: No, the idea of unconditional love as an important divine theme came in with the prophetic religions -- Christianity has one, Buddhism has one, and so on. Why not pray to the gods you worship and ask for their guidance?

replies.title: Re: Some polytheistic questions!
replies.content:

I'm not sure if I trust my own perception of answers to some of these things. There are times when ideas or images come quite out of the blue during rituals, which feel like something to heed. But other things where for months I really feel like doing something, instinctively, in rituals, but it goes contrary to advice from very experienced people like yourself. There'll be a reason people like you were giving that advice.

replies.title: Re: Some polytheistic questions!
replies.content: The advice not to mix pantheons is advice, not a commandment handed down from on high, and it's primarily aimed at modern people who want to assemble a hodgpodge of deities for reasons not that far from fashion-consciousness. If you really feel you need to include the worship of Jesus in among the worship of other deities from a different background, talk to the deities involved, make sure they're cool with it, and see where it takes you.

comments.title: Magic in Other Languages
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

I remember reading or hearing you say that one of the best ways for occult writers to publish new material is to translate the magical texts of other languages. To your knowledge, what European languages have the greatest potential for exploring new magical material that is currently unknown to (or not actively being explored by) English speakers?

replies.title: Re: Magic in Other Languages
replies.content:
Every single language in the world other than English. I mean that quite literally. Pick a living language, any living language, and at this point there's more occult material available in that language that has never been seen in English than you will have time to translate if you start now and keep translating until the day you die. The really big fish are French, Spanish, German, Latin, and Russian, but I bet you could find a lifetime's worth of occult translations in Estonian, Welsh, or Romansh without too much difficulty!

The thing to keep in mind is that the English language occult scene these days is incredibly insular. Most people literally have no idea that there's any kind of modern occultism other than what was invented in England between the founding of the Golden Dawn and whenever Gerald Gardner finished manufacturing the first Book of Shadows -- it's all rehashes and recombinations of a very narrow stock of ideas, with occasional borrowings from ancient sources. France was the center of occultism in Europe from the 1850s until after the Second World War -- how much material from French sources has been put into English? A pittance. I have yet to see a single book by Stanislaus de Guaita, Josephin Peladan, Robert Ambelain, etc., etc., translated into English -- it's embarrassing. Germany and the other German-speaking regions? Even less -- in English pretty much all we've got is Franz Bardon, Georg Lomer, and a few figures in the runic revival of the early 20th century. I could go on at vast length. If you're interested, pick a language, get a solid reading knowledge (to the point that you can pick up a book and read a few pages without more than the occasional glance at a dictionary), and be ready to have your sense of what magic is and what it can do stretched beyond all recognition.

replies.title: Re: Magic in Other Languages
replies.content: Are there any untranslated works in the German language that are worth looking at? Particularly any that may somehow be related to Druidry?

replies.title: Re: Magic in Other Languages
replies.content: Yes, as I noted above, there's a vast amount of German magical literature that nobody in the English-speaking world knows anything about; my German isn't good enough to allow me to review what's out there and recommend anything, though. For the same reason, you'd have to ask someone with a good reading knowledge of German what German-language Druid literature is like -- there's a big German Druid scene, and has been for a century and a half, but I don't know what's been published.

replies.title: Re: Magic in Other Languages
replies.content: And than there is the problem that in today's world, it is, despite the internet, not easy to know which books are out there and if they are good, because occultism is such a small subculture. At least, interestingly, the books are as a rule not very expensive. In one big bookstore in Berlin in the aisle for esoteric/new-age books there was only one book about magic (it was something about white magic, probably something Neopagan); the rest was all the usual fluffy Stuff in the vein of "The Secret".

replies.title: Re: Magic in Other Languages
replies.content:
In response to booklover1973: I would suggest that many of the more culturally valuable opportunities are in translating works that may not necessarily be in a big city bookshop but do happen to be in the public domain, that is, out of copyright, published in the early 20th century, 19th century, and earlier. (Commercial works by living authors, that gets into sticky wickets of agents & contracts, etc.) Much of what a translator adds in value is not just the translation; it is in introducing the book-- telling us why it merited a translation, why it merits reading. Yes, this takes a lot of time and effort. And alas, on a per hour basis one could make three times more working at a car wash in January in Siberia, probably. Hence, we have so very few translations. But for those who can and will do this work it does have its rewards.
A TRANSLATOR

replies.title: Re: Magic in Other Languages
replies.content: On an hourly basis, yeah, I wouldn't be at all surprised if it's about a third of the going rate for carwash employees in Siberia. On the other hand, if you can take what you learn and rewrite it in a form that's more accessible to modern readers -- cough, cough, my geomancy books, cough, cough -- that can be rather more lucrative.

replies.title: Re: Magic in Other Languages
replies.content:
Dear John Michael Greer,
Glad to see this question and your answer. As a translator myself I can say that, yes indeed, there is a lot of low-hanging fruit for translators-- plus a field or eleven of mega-watermelons. But it does take some effort to figure out which works merit the trouble, and then actually do the translation, and then see it into print. I for one am most appreciative of your efforts in this regard.
Kind regards,
MILLICENTLY LURKING

I read it as saying that the collaboration will be successful and of benefit to me, and along the way I’ll have to give up or change some behavioral patterns that have been keeping me from moving into the kind of work that I’d like to be doing.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Er, you have two very negative figures and one positive figure in the court, and you read that as positive?

No, Carcer as right witness shows that you're considering this because you feel "stuck" or otherwise restricted in your life or musical career. (The Way of Points goes up to Carcer again as second mother, showing that this is tied into some broader feeling of "stuckness" in your life.) Amissio as Judge shows that the effort you put into this will be poured down a rathole and will amount to nothing. Fortuna Major as left witness shows that in the long run -- Fortuna Major very often means success after delay -- you'll benefit from it in some way, just not any time soon, and not in the way you hope.

The reason most people fail at divination is that they try to get the oracle to tell them what they want to hear, rather than listening to what it's saying. In a geomancy reading, when you do a shield chart and two or more of the court figures (judge and witnesses) are negative figures, that means no. If you try to make it say yes, you'll feel better in the short term, but your reading will be wrong, and you'll land yourself in trouble you could have avoided.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
An update:

With your reply about the map, I was able to verify a number of things. Sadly, the story is a bit complex, and there is no way to make a short version; I will present you some points I found after a couple of hours searching.

First, there is the original research made by Hapgood, that sent a copy of Finaeus 1513 map to the US Air Force in 1960. I found a copy of the response at
http://www.aurorasky.net/Oronteus_Finaeus_Map.pdf
which is the source for the Finaeus map corresponding to a copy of and Antarctica map with it ice-free and using a different, complex projection.

To correspond to Antarctica, the continent, as depicted in Finaeus map, would need to be rotated a bit. It seems that Fineaus rotated and scaled Antarctica in his map to make it fit with the Gulf of Carpentaria in Australia, as shown in
https://web.archive.org/web/20120503220916/http://www.diegocuoghi.it/Piri_Reis/Finaeus_eng.htm

However, this explains the rotation, not the source of the map. Wikipedia's article on Finaeus was pointing to the dead site above
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Oronce_Fin%C3%A9

The Wikipedia article states that Finaeus was in contact with Johannes Schöner, who used the same Antarctica map on his 1524 globe.

And Graham Hancock provides an excellent analysis of Schöner's (and, by extension, Finaeus') map on his site:
https://grahamhancock.com/fisherd1/

I would advise to check the links I provided; I, myself, will do this when I have some free time.

Talismans as a type of machine? I ask as I'm meditating on this theme for a piece I'm working on at the moment, and the recent secondary literature isn't the most helpful here, since it almost invariably assumes that magic can only ever be symbolically efficacious, never "actually," and hence magic can never really be tech.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I tend to argue that magic is the opposite of technology. To borrow a distinction from Martin Buber, technology is based entirely on "I-it" relationships -- you manuipulate a thing and it does what you want. Magic is based entirely on "I-you" relationships -- it requires you to participate in an interaction and to bring your whole self to the meeting. When magic is done from an I-it standpoint it becomes cacomagic, and destructive.

1) The red bag doesn't just stop malicious magic. It also stops less conscious forms of nasty energy. Lots of people who don't think they practice magic project hatred and other emotions toward people they dislike -- and there's a lot of that going on just now, of course.

2) What you've descreived is an ordinary course of study in Hermetic occultism. You should be fine.

3) No, it's not correct -- far from it. One of the things that puzzles teachers of alchemy is that so many beginners want to make a beeline for fungi. Fungi are organisms of decay and the energy they concentrate is death-energy -- that's why so many of them are lethally poisonous. Green plants, by contrast, are organisms of growth and life and the energy they concentrate is life-energy. Fungi unquestionably have their place -- the circle of the cosmos includes death as well as life -- but spagyric preparations made with fungi are not something you want to mess with. At all.

comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Hope you are well. I have a few unrelated questions today.

1. How does one alter actions based on results of a divination, but still learn from the divination by comparing the events of the day later on? It seems like then events won't be as "in line" with the initial reading due to changing your actions based on the reading, and the events of the day would not align with it. I haven't had much experience with this as I haven't tried to alter my daily actions based on readings yet. I just do the reading in the morning, forget about it for the rest of th day, and then compare the events of the day the next morning.


2. Do you think its worth writing down possible past life memories or exploring them further if one is not actively seeking them? A week or so ago, I had a random vision pop into my head while meditating on the path of Shin. I saw myself as a young boy in a winter setting and got an impression of his life as he got older. I'm not sure if it's anything meaningful or not. Should I explore it further, or just wait and see if anything comes up again spontaneously? I also had a few other things that seem suggestive of past life memories but I worry about being too eager to consider them such in case I'll create false memories. An instance that sticks in my mind is during a physics class when I randomly realized in a lab that I knew how an electrical circuit works despite not having covered that information yet, and the instructor confirmed I was correct when I asked him.


3. I've had impressions of late where I sort of "see the whole/big picture" of a situation, or realize how things fit together (for example, suddenly having an understanding of factors affecting my parents' financial situation, or understanding certain habits I have), or gain sudden flashes of understanding where a concept or symbol becomes clear to me at a deeper level and I don't have to think of every individual part separately, but rather I see how each part works as a whole (such as a sudden flash of insight when meditating on a path of the Tree, and seeing how it influences the energy dynamics of the Tree as a whole rather than just as an individual symbol separated from the whole). Is this how the mental body communicates with the lower levels of the self, or are these flashes something else entirely? They seem useful to me, but aren't consistent and I feel like getting the important information written down right away after one of these flashes is necessary or else the clarity quickly dissipates and I don't feel that sense of "seeing the big picture" anymore.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Keep doing it the way you're doing it, and don't worry about changing your behavior for now. You'll find that down the road a bit, your divinations start offering you advice -- "do this and that will happen" -- and you can follow that and judge the results. Over the long run, you'll find that the divination helps you see the whole picture, and you can choose your path within that without overturning the divination's message.

2) Definitely write it down, but don't try to dig up anything that doesn't surface spontaneously. Over time, if they're real memories, the rest will become clear.

3) Good -- that's an excellent sign; you're beginning to perceive glimpses of the mental plane. Definitely write them down so you can hold onto as much as possible of the insights.
